Samsung released the Galaxy ZFold 7, an 8-in tablet that folds in half into something that resembles a phone, just thinner. Both had chips that were as fast as any iPhone and came with great cameras. But when they looked at what the old guard and Certino had been doing with the iPhone, many thought, "This is boring." Then September arrived and Apple debuted the iPhone 17 Pro. The phone is Apple's answer to those boring accusations. It's the company's Roger Daltry Scream, silencing critics. But there wasn't any fancy over-the-top AI or innovative folding screen. This was Apple going back to what it does best, hardware. They took a 5,000 milliamp hour battery, stuffed it inside a new aluminum unibody frame, and took the iPhone square bump and stretched it wide across the back of the phone like Jack Nicholson's Joker smile in Batman. To top it all off, Apple made it a bold, bright, nearly electric orange color, loud enough to make anyone forget when Apple's phones were boring. The iPhone 17 Pro isn't delicate or polite. It's a bolt of thunder that can also order you an Uber. It is a mean looking phone, which is actually part of its charm. Yeah, sure, you could try to hide that meanness in a calm dark blue color, but it's still an absolute beast, just in a Tom Ford suit. Where Motorola and Samsung made their phones look futuristic and fun with different fabrics, Apple's iPhone 17 Pro is decidedly old school. Now, some people won't like the 17 Pro's aggressive looks and think it's too big, too heavy, too similar to iPhone models previously, but that changes the moment you unlock the phone. The new iOS 26 interface is clean, contemporary, and has the kind of shiny translucent accents that you'd find in an Italian nightclub that plays trashy EDM. Messages finally grew up and got more customizable with the addition of background images for group chats and polls that helped keep a thread's discussion about dinner plans from turning into a long messy chat. You can have any number of lock screens with any number of cats and make them do this. When you open the camera app, more changes are revealed. The selfie camera got better and lets you take horizontal snaps when holding the phone vertically. It can also automatically zoom out to let more people in the frame. The telephoto camera has a 4x magnification lens that captures fantastic photos. It doesn't quite get you as close to your subject as the iPhone 16 Pro did, but it takes images with more detail and better dynamic range. 8x photos look incredible as long as the lighting is decent. And then there's the battery. In everyday use, the Pro and Pro Max last a day on a single charge. And in CNET's battery test, the Pro models did well. The 17 Pro Max has the best battery life of any phone we've ever tested, iPhone or Android. And if you're someone who's obsessively online, the 17 Pro Max is the one to go with. It's your best shot at quenching your constant use needs. The 17 Pro did well, too. Better than the 16 Pro and as good as the 16 Pro Max in our 45 minutes endurance test. But if you're considering a Pro, it's worth looking at the iPhone 17. For $270 less, the iPhone 17 did just as well as the iPhone 17 Pro did in our test. In fact, in our 3-hour streaming video test, the standard 17 lasted longer than the 17 Pro. And the comparisons go beyond battery life. The two phones have the same screen, the same selfie camera, the same ceramic shield 2 on the front, the same IP68 rating for dust and water resistance, and more. And while the Pro has a more powerful processor on paper, I think the reason to go Pro is for the vapor cooling chamber, which allows the A19 Pro chip to go harder for gaming and I imagine helps keep the phone future proof. The Pro also has a dedicated telephoto lens that the 17 doesn't, but is that worth paying $270 more? That's a question you're going to have to answer. Undercutting all of this were the gates, scratch gate, where demo units inside the Apple store had marks and scratches on the back. Now, Apple told CNET that those weren't scratches, but material transferred from aging MagSafe display units. And then there was Zach Nelson and his jerryrig everything video that honed in on the iPhone 17 Pro's camera plateau and how the steep edges made it easy for Apple's hard anodized coating to chip off. Now, Apple told CNET that normal wear and tear might lead to small abrasions. Adding to all this is CNET's very own Jeffrey Msrai, who noticed a static noise coming out of the iPhone 17 Pro Max's speaker when the phone's volume was set to zero and plugged in to a wired USBC charger. Now, reports on Reddit, Apple support, and Mac rumors forums agree with his findings. Ms. Rahi described the sound as being like a walkietalkie noise. Now, this could be digital interference maybe, but it doesn't seem to be widespread. Lastly, there are all the accessories. Apple has a new tech woven case, which is so far in my use handling so much better than the fine woven cases for the iPhone 15 series. I also like Peak Design's Everyday Case, which has support for MagSafe and Peak Designs ecosystem. I really love the vegan leather looks. I also tested several Beats phone cases, including the rugged case and my favorite, the Beats kickstand case, which has a wrist strap that turns into a mini stand. Now, as a cat owner, I've had to remove the wrist strap because, well, my cat thinks it's a toy. And if you're a filmmaker or someone shooting video, especially multicam, there's the Black Magagic Camera Pro Dock. Now, I just got this in before filming this video, so you have to forgive me for not having a proper cage for it. But this dock gives you separate audio in-n-out ports, USBC 3 and two ports, HDMI, gen lock, and has two tripod mounts. But perhaps my favorite accessory is this one from Google. It's the Pixel Snap ringstand, which acts like a pop sockety way to hold the phone, but also a stand that's great for video calls, watching video, or even as a tripod in a pench. After a month with the iPhone 17 Pro and 17 Pro Max, I still think both are outstanding phones and show that Apple is still listening to its customers and offering them innovative features that they want, but doing it in a way that Apple only can. If you have an iPhone 13 Pro or older, it's definitely worth pondering an upgrade. And if you're someone who still has a previous smaller Pro model, it's worth giving the iPhone 17 a serious look. title: Why Orange iPhones are turning Pink?? #iphone17promax #apple
transcript: What has happened that the iPhones which were in Cosmic Orange color are changing color like chameleons and now they are appearing in this color. First of all, this feature is available only in a few iPhones.  That too only in cosmic orange colour. Orange Cosmic Orange has arrived. And as far as why this is happening is concerned, there could be two possible reasons behind it.  The first is manufacturing defect.  Look, the aluminum frame of iPhones is anodized before being painted. Due to which some microscopic pores are formed on the surface of the frame and it is because of these pores that coloring of the frame becomes possible. After coloring, sealing is done to fill the pores of the surface. Due to which the color of the frame remains preserved for a long time.  So there is a possibility that the sealing process of some batch of iPhones may not have been done properly. Due to which this color change is being seen. Or the second reason could be that users are repeatedly cleaning the iPhone with a solution that contains hydrogen peroxide or bleach. Because Apple itself prohibits the use of such solutions. title: IT'S TOO GOOD! | iPhone 17 Review 1 Month Later!
transcript: This is what happens when Apple stops messing up. We end up getting a killer phone that in many ways feels like a pro. Whether it's the great display, the solid performance, or even the new cameras. Last year, I hated the iPhone 16. A lot of AI slop around the action button that it's worthless. But I finally think they woke up. So, here are my top five reasons why I think the iPhone 17 is the best phone of the year after using it for a month. First off, is now a bit bigger with a 6.3 in display. I'm a fan of small phones and I think the size is just right. And you know what? It feels like it's worth the price because now it has an LTPO panel that goes from 1 to 120 Hz. And that's exactly why it supports the always on display. Now, I think this shouldn't be a big deal because we literally have $200 Android phones with screen at 120 Hz. But they give this one the same display we have on the Pros. Even the anti-reflective coating is there, though. I mean, it's not quite on par with the Galaxy S25 Ultra. Still, the fact that they're not just holding back on features to sell the Pro, I mean, it feels like something Apple wouldn't do. The second reason, performance. And to my surprise, it actually beats my iPhone 16 Pro in some benchmarks I've run. And with the upgraded 256 GB of storage, it feels a bit more fair at this price point. It definitely runs hotter than the iPhone 17 Pro Max like you're seeing right now, but honestly, I haven't noticed any heat during a daily use, and I don't miss using my old phone. Everything just flows like it should. Sure, it's not an ideal phone for gaming, and it doesn't pack as much RAM as the Pros, but still, it feels as a powerful device that will last me several years. The third reason is the battery life. Coming from the Pro Max, I mean, I can feel the difference. With the Pro, I would end the day with 30% and this one cutting it pretty close by at that point. But at least it charges faster, hitting 50% in just 20 minutes. And you know what's funny is that this is the cheapest phone you can get with Gi 2.2. So yeah, you can wirelessly charge it at 25 W. This year, we also got new cameras, especially the new 48 MP ultrawide and also a new 18 megapixel front-facing camera that now lets you switch to a horizontal field of view and shoot 4K at 60 frames per second like that. Meanwhile, with the ultrawide, we can finally take micro shots using this sensor. The main sensor is the same that we had on the iPhone 17. So, you would notice a lot of differences, but the ultra wide and the selfie camera are the same that we have on the iPhones Pro, which led me to my final point and probably the most important in this review. Apple finally focus on what matters, and you can really tell it with the hardware upgrades. I mean, they're meaningful. No talk about the action button or Apple intelligence. No redesign either because they didn't needed one. Instead of fluff, they improve on what really counts. a clear revolution that even makes it more appealing than the Pros. I know a bunch of people that were planning to go for the Pro just for the 120 Hz display and the extra storage, but now I mean they don't have any reason to. The iPhone 17 is selling for $7.99, while the Pro, I mean, it costs $300 more. And the craziest part of all is that for this price point, it's even a better choice hardware-wise that some new Android phones like the Pixel 10. This phone has the same size and screen brightness, but it's not LTPO. Its performance is also way behind the iPhone 17, not to mention the storage in terms of charging speed is a bit slower, too. And all of this while costing the same as the iPhone 17. I just think it's wild that Google launched a phone with worse specs than the base iPhone 17. I mean, Google is behaving more like Apple than [music] Apple itself. But even then, there's still a couple of things that Apple could improve, like the USB connection. I mean, 2.0, it's [music] just not it at this price point. And a 12 megapixel telephoto lens like the ones we had in the old iPhone Pros would have been great, especially since other phones have it now. But even with that, I mean, this is just a great phone. And honestly, it feels too good for Apple. I kind of feel that this is a glitch in the system. And after the failure of the iPhone 17 and Apple Intelligence, Apple had to do something drastic, you know, and this year they focused on hardware upgrades. They caught all the fluff and this is what we got. Probably the best iPhone they had launched in years. And I really doubt that we will see a huge improvement with the iPhone 18. So, I mean, just get it. I just think this is a great phone and I really think it's the best phone of the year. But anyway, that's just my opinion. And if you have a different one, you can leave it in the comments. I'll see you in the next one.

title: iPhone 17 Pro Max vs iPhone Air - This was surprising!
transcript: I've now spent a considerable amount of time using both of these phones and I've come to a conclusion. Now, bear in mind that everyone has different needs and that's why Apple designs multiple variations of the iPhone each year. Some want a lowcost device while others want the most cuttingedge features and performance. I've done a day in the life review with both of these phones and if you want to check those out, I'll [music] leave a link in the pop out banner. In this video, I'll be comparing the iPhone 17 Pro Max with the iPhone Air and giving you my honest opinion on which one I would pick. Let's start off with the design. Now, I wouldn't say that the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max are necessarily ugly phones. They're still decent looking phones, but definitely not one of Apple's best designs just in terms of how it looks. I had a look at the orange and midnight blue versions at the Apple store, and honestly, the silver was a no-brainer. I genuinely think that they made an orange phone specifically for marketing purposes. It may be to some people's tastes, but I feel like I'd get sick of it pretty quickly. But my issue with the 17 Pro is actually the two-tone [music] design. Even though they've tried to make things as symmetrical as possible, something about this two-tone look just throws off the elegance. [music] It's a lot more subtle with the silver model, and chances are you'll probably be putting a case on your phone anyway, but I just don't like it. [music] It's pretty obvious that Apple has prioritized function with the 17 Pro and Pro Max [music] and prioritized form with the iPhone Air. Now, that's not to say that the iPhone Air is lacking in functionality and performance, [music] and we'll get to that in just a second. I will say this, the iPhone Air is arguably the best looking iPhone Apple has ever designed. They've pushed a lot of the internal components closer to the top around the camera plateau, which has allowed them to create the thinnest iPhone ever. [music] The majority of the phone here is actually just the battery. They've stuck with the titanium frame and used the glass panel on the back which creates this really clean looking back finish. The magic actually happens though when you hold the phone in your hand. And it's hard to describe but they've managed to achieve the right mix of thickness, weight, and balance, which makes the iPhone Air feel extremely premium in your hand. Now, naturally, when people first saw how thin this phone was, a lot of people were skeptical about whether the phone would bend easily. There's been a lot of tests done now, and it's pretty clear that with everyday use, it's almost impossible to bend this phone. Now, no matter how hard I try, the phone barely even flexes. And that's actually quite impressive for a device this thin. So, let's talk about camera systems. There's no question the 17 Pro and Pro Max have the superior camera systems, and this year the cameras on the Pros are actually quite something special. All three sensors are 48 megapixels, and you can now reach 8 times optical zoom. I'm sure by now you would have seen some videos on social media of people zooming right into a subject with that eight time zoom and then zooming all the way back to.5. I've done it a few times now and it's really impressive. With the Pro models, you can of course take photos in Pro RAW and shoot in Apple log, but from my day- in the life review, doing so actually drains the battery significantly. Now, this makes sense because it takes a lot more power to actually capture higher quality images. So, even though the iPhone Pro models have bigger batteries, if you're constantly taking the highest quality images possible, you will be using up that battery faster. With the iPhone Air, you do get just the one 48 megapixel rear camera with a two times digital zoom. The photos are still really good. It's just not as versatile as the Pro models. The 48 MP sensor on the Air is not the exact same as the Pro's main sensor, but if you're mainly taking photos for Instagram and other social media platforms, [music] you likely won't even be able to tell the difference. But the feature that really sets this year's lineup apart is the 18 megapixel front-facing [music] camera with center stage. Now, both of these phones have those features, and I use these features on both phones, and I really enjoyed it. Being able to hold your phone vertically while taking a horizontal video just makes things so much more comfortable. Holding your phone horizontally with one hand is just sort of awkward for me. So, this is a much welcomed feature. As for the performance, they both use the same A19 Pro chips, but the Pro models have the vapor chamber cooling system and an aluminium body. Now, the whole point of those features is to keep the chip cool so that it can run at peak performance for longer. The iPhone Air does not have those features. So, for performance inensive tasks, the Pro model iPhones will no doubt handle them far better. Now, from my day in the life with the iPhone Air, I realized that the 6.5 in screen is actually the sweet spot for me. It's not too large and it's not too small. I was able to comfortably take a meeting from my iPhone Air and read everything that my software developer was showing me while he was sharing his screen. This year, all the iPhones in the lineup get a 120 Hz display with 3,000 nits of peak brightness. And this was really helpful out in the sun. So then what's the verdict? Well, these two phones are actually far more similar than I expected in terms of a dayto-day experience for me. Personally for me, I actually don't do very performance-intensive tasks on my iPhone. I do my video editing on my MacBook and I generally don't play any games on my phone, so my workload on my iPhone is actually pretty limited. I watch a lot of videos. I use some social media apps, FaceTime, and take a few photos here and there. So, even though the iPhone Air has the smaller battery, for my needs, it's actually the perfect phone, [music] I have a professional camera that I take with me if I know I'm going on a trip or to an event where I want to take highquality photos. So, I don't actually need to rely on my iPhone. But, of course, if you are someone that does play a lot of games, you do want to take highquality photos on your phone, then [music] go with the Pro. There's no question. But if you're like me and you value having a good-looking, light [music] phone, then the iPhone Air might be the one for you. If you enjoyed this video, give it a like. And if you want to see more videos like this one, be sure to hit that subscribe button below. Thanks so much for watching. I'll see you in the next video. [music]

title: ProRes ‘Almost’ RAW vs 12-Bit DCG RAW Video Shootout! (iPhone 17 Pro vs Pixel 10 Pro)
transcript: In this video, I'm going to share my findings  as to which is the best device for filmmakers,   the iPhone 17 in ProRes RAW or the Google  Pixel 10 Pro in 12-bit DCG RAW. The answer   is both more complicated and surprising than I'd  originally thought. From image quality, autofocus   and stabilization to ProRes RAW versus MotionCam  RAW workflows, file sizes, storage requirements,   accessories, durability, and more. This is the  ultimate deep dive you've been waiting for. First, let's start by addressing the  elephant in the room, ProRes RAW, which   is the industry-leading Apple developed video  codec that records all the information captured   by the camera sensor, provide unparalleled  flexibility in post-prouction. Like me,   you probably expected the iPhone 17 Pro to deliver  on the promise of raw video. And by raw video,   I mean unprocessed data capture directly from  the camera's sensor, devoid of any additional   image signal processing or ISP. And we didn't get  that. Apple still applies a ton of processing to   the data coming off the sensor before encoding it  to ProRes RAW. This means your noise reduction,   vignette correction, even enhanced dynamic range  via frame stacking is all still present. Is it   better than standard ProRes available on the 15  and 16 Pro? Yes, a bit. Does it deliver a great   image? Mostly, but is it raw video? No, it's not.  A better name is ProRes almost raw. Of course,   Apple baking in a ton of ISP has some  benefits like sparing you denoising in post,   but that defeats the whole purpose of raw video,  which should offer full control over your image in   post thanks to untouch raw sensor data, something  the Pixel 10 Pro delivers in spades using Motion   Cam Pro. So, how does ProRes RAW compare to true  12-bit raw video on the Pixel 10 Pro? Unlike the   iPhone motion cam pro captures the raw sensor  data and saves it in its proprietary MC RAW   video format, you can then convert this to DNG  raw for editing. This bypasses all the phone's   image signal processing and delivers a true raw  video format which is simply delightful. Whereas   ProRes RAW allows for ISO, exposure bias, detail,  noise, and temperature adjustments in Resolve,   RAW video from the Pixel 10 Pro offers a much more  comprehensive suite of camera raw adjustments like   highlight and shadow controls, saturation and  contrast, sharpness, and most impressively,   highlight recovery. This allows us to clip the  green channel at time of recording and restore   the image information from the unclipped red  and blue channels, which is incredibly useful   and delivers amazing dynamic range. On the iPhone  17 Pro, however, once your highlights are clipped,   they are gone forever. You can't retrieve that  information as ProRes RAW on the iPhone does   not support highlight recovery. And while the  iPhone certainly delivers reduced sharpening   in ProRes RAW versus non-Apple log formats,  it still appears to receive some additional   sharpening. Look how much more organic the  Pixel 10 looks with sharpness set to zero   versus the iPhone 17 Pro. And let's take a quick  look at EDR in play on the iPhone 17 Pro. If you   film in a high dynamic range scene with very  bright highlights with exposure set to auto,   we get dynamic range that is too good to be true.  That is until we lock exposure. You can see that   the frame stacking HDR ISP is suddenly disabled  and we instantly lose our highlight detail. Clear   proof that this is anything but raw video. On the  one hand, this is kind of a cool feature. Extra   dynamic range in auto mode, but it is bad for  consistency and should not be a feature of raw   video. And the other thing that struck me when  comparing the image out of both smartphones,   is how much richer the color information is on  the Pixel, especially in more subtle gradients   like the sky at dusk or in high contrast scenes  that feature a lot of highlight information. The   iPhone just doesn't capture everything that was  actually available to it. And it isn't just a   matter of increasing saturation either. The Pixel  consistently captures higher quality images that   are more representative of what I perceive with  my eye while filming. And lastly, let's compare   the file size of RAW in Motion Cam Pro verse  ProRes RAW and ProRes RAW HQ on the iPhone. A 1   minute recording on the Pixel 10 Pro comes in at  approximately 15 GB in MC RAW format. When this   is converted to the RAW DGs, the same clip is  19 GB when ready to edit. On the iPhone 17 Pro,   the same minute long recording clocks in at 7.88  GB in ProRes RAW and 12 GB in ProRes RAW HQ,   making the iPhone much more efficient for file  size. But the downside is there is less data to   work with, and it shows. So, when we compare the  raw video on the iPhone 17 Pro versus the Google   Pixel 10 Pro, the Pixel is the clear winner in  this round when it comes to pure image quality. Now, let's compare the quality of the glass on the  iPhone 17 Pro versus the Pixel 10 Pro. On paper,   the two devices stack up pretty closely with the  iPhone offering a 13 mm ultra wide lens, the 24 mm   main lens and a 100 mm telephoto lens, all using  48 megapixel sensors. The Pixel 10 Pro features a   48 MP 13 mm ultra-wide, a 50 megapixel 25mm main  lens, and a 48 megapixel 114 mm telephoto lens.   Both main lenses are excellent. The ultra-wide  on the Pixel is prone to heavier chromatic   aberration on the corners than the iPhone, and  the telly lenses are close, but I'd give the Pixel   the edge. It is 14 mm longer than the iPhone,  supports a much closer minimum focus distance,   and delivers gorgeous results. But the Pixel has  another trick up its sleeve thanks to MotionCam   Pro. Unlike on the iPhone 17 Pro, which does not  support in sensor zoom to provide additional focal   lengths in ProRes RAW, the Pixel 10 Pro delivers  a 51 mm and 228 mm lens, which is achieved by   cropping in on the sensor and has Apple improved  their signature lens flare, which has long   plagued the iPhone. Yes, the lens coating is much  improved on the 17 Pro. And what's more, Google   has achieved the impressive feat of beating Apple  when it comes to ugly lens flares, delivering a   pronounced green goblin on the main lens when  filming bright, undiffused light sources. Now, when it comes to image stabilization,  there is a clear winner here, the Pixel 10   Pro. It offers optical image stabilization on  all rear lenses when capturing in open gate raw,   whereas the iPhone 17 Pro disables  stabilization on all lenses in ProRes   RAW. Not even sensor shift is available on  the iPhone. Handheld, this is literally the   difference between getting usable footage and  shots only your trash can will get to enjoy,   especially on the telly lens, which  is simply unusable on the iPhone. When it comes to low light performance, you  could say this is where the iPhone's almost RAW   with heavy noise reduction performed in camera  really shines. You can get an incredibly clean   image straight out of camera, but of course,  you're deprived of the ability to denoise in   post using something like Resolve or Neat Video.  The Pixel 10 Pro is no slouch either here, thanks   to DCG being enabled on the sensor when using the  main wide lens, and you get very good low light   performance and a lot more color information  in the shadows when compared to the iPhone.   But as you would expect, there is a lot more noise  present as this is free from denoising. It's hard   to pick a winner here because you're comparing  apples to oranges. It comes down to whether you   want full control over denoising in post or a  super clean image out of camera. Both are strong   performers here with the Pixel giving the most  pure image with more chroma information intact. Next, let's talk about some key specs as  they pertain to video such as frame rates,   sustain performance, and thermals. And let's start  with resolution. On the iPhone 17 Pro, we get a   slightly higher max resolution of 4224x3024. And  on the Pixel 10 Pro, we get 4080x3072. ProRes RAW   is only supported when capturing to an external  SSD on the iPhone, which is a key limitation,   whereas RAW capture on the Pixel is supported  both internally and externally to an SSD, which   is great. When it comes to record times, I've  successfully recorded over 1 hour of 4K ProRes   RAW HQ at 120 fps to my external SSD, and no doubt  it would go for much longer if I had the patience.   On the iPhone, you're simply limited by available  storage on your SSD. On the Pixel 10 Pro, when   recording externally to the same SSD, I can record  continuously for a max of about 20 minutes in RAW   before I get dropped frames and IO bottlenecks in  cool conditions. While filming this video, I've   had dropped frames when recording internally after  just 5 minutes. So, ambient temperatures play a   big role in the sort of performance you can expect  from the Pixel. For extended record times, the   iPhone 17 Pro is the clear winner. When it comes  to max supported frame rates, again, the iPhone 17   Pro wins here with support for 60 fps in open gate  and 120 fps in 4K. The Pixel 10 Pro is limited to   a max of 30 fps in OpenGate at 4K and 120 fps  at 1080p in 10- bit RAW without DCG. However,   it will drop frames after 5 to 10 seconds, so it  isn't a reliable high-speed filmmaking option.   Something else to be aware of on the Pixel 10 Pro  is the laser detect autofocus module positioned   directly next to the wide lens. This is constantly  firing and will be reflected on close objects and   more problematically on your ND filter or lens.  This means you'll need to cover it with Blu-Tack   or electrical tape if you intend to use an ND  filter. The iPhone 17 doesn't have this issue,   nor does any other Android device I've tested.  And speaking of autofocus, even when the LDAF is   uncovered on the Pixel 10 Pro, it still performs  significantly worse than the iPhone, which does a   much better job. Unless you are dead center in the  frame, the Pixel 10 just won't focus on you. So,   where possible on the Pixel, make sure you use  manual focus, and of course, Motion Cam Pro   supports focus peaking to nail this. But if you're  a one-man band like yours truly that relies on   autofocus, the iPhone is the winner here, too. And  when it comes to general performance, the Pixel is   a serious underachiever. Even in just mildly warm  conditions, the CPU will throttle after just 5 to   10 minutes of use when filming in raw, which leads  to instabilities in capture. The iPhone 17 Pro,   even the 15 Pro actually, offers flawless sustain  performance even in really warm environments. That   puts the Pixel to shame. And lastly, what makes  the Pixel 10 Pro so special is it's the first   phone to unlock dual conversion gain or DCG on the  sensor out of the box. I've made a whole video on   why this is amazing that you can watch, but just  think of this as dual output gain that you'll find   on high-end cinema cameras where it can capture  two analog exposures simultaneously and merge them   for improved noise and expanded dynamic range.  But not to be confused with HDR frame stacking,   there is no ghosting present with DCG. So in this  regard, the Pixel 10 Pro is in a league of its   own. But due to all the other issues I've just  outlined, imagine what would happen if Xiaomi,   Samsung, or Vivo unlock DCG on their sensors  without the performance issues that plague   the Pixel. So, when it comes to supportive frame  rates, sustained record times, and performance in   general, the iPhone 17 Pro absolutely destroys the  Pixel in this round when it comes to key specs. When it comes to film making accessories like  cases, tripod mounts, ND filters, and lenses,   the iPhone 17 Pro is the clear winner by a country  mile. In fact, no manufacturer currently makes a   dedicated solution for the Pixel 10 Pro, although  I hope that will change in the future. On the   iPhone, we get dozens of options from companies  like Mman, Small Rig, and my personal favorite,   Freewell. This makes rigging out your phone easy  with professional gear like their hybrid V&D   filter solution that even supports drop-in filters  with the Pro Filmmaker case. On the Pixel 10 Pro,   we are currently limited to universal solutions.  The best of them comes from NIWA. I use it with a   67mm magnetic step-up ring with the Freewell M2  ND filter kit and Freewell lenses, and it gets   the job done, but is only available on AliExpress  right now. So, while you can rig out your Pixel   10 Pro effectively, it doesn't come close to the  iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max on the accessory front. No matter how great an image you can pull from  your camera, a major factor in choosing the   right device is workflow. How quickly and easily  can you go from capture to import, edit, grade,   and upload? And for myself at least, this is where  the iPhone 17 Pro shines thanks to ProRes RAW.   Despite its ISP limitations and all that comes  with that, the fact that you can capture directly   to an SSD, then simply connect it to your  computer and start editing with nothing   else to do is one of the key reasons you might  choose the 17 Pro over the Google Pixel. With   Motion Cam Pro Raw on the Pixel, you will need  to perform the extra step of converting the MC   Raw video files to DNG. This certainly isn't as  quick and easy as ProRes RAW, but it is true RAW   and brings all of the imaging benefits and in my  opinion is well worth the extra steps for projects   where image quality takes priority over speed.  But in the time it takes to just export my RAW   files from Motion Cam Pro ready for editing, I  could have finished a full edit with ProRes RAW   and published to YouTube. So for fast turnarounds,  the iPhone 17 Pro's ProRes RAW wins for workflow. Another key consideration for filmmakers is  durability and reliability of these devices.   If you're treating them as cameras instead  of phones, you need to be sure they can   withstand the many perils of filmm from knocks  to drops. And should they sustain any damage,   have them repaired without it negatively impacting  your shooting schedule too much. Both my iPhone   and Pixel have taken a drop. My iPhone sank  to the bottom of the Hobart waterfront after I   tripped and flicked it out of the tripod clamp.  It remained submerged for over 3 hours, 10 ft   below in freezing ocean waters while I went and  purchased a powerful magnet and magnet fish for it   for over an hour before reeling it in. Thankfully,  the Freewell Pro Filmmakaker's magnetic lens mount   meant I retrieved it without issue. an hour to dry  out and all ports and functionality was perfect   like it never happened. Had it been completely  fooled, my Apple Care would have resulted in a   full device replacement with no questions asked.  Or if damage was minor, a repair in store at my   local Apple shop in Hobart, at worst, I'd be out  of action for 1 to 4 days. Now, compare this with   the Pixel 10 Pro. This got blown on a tripod  by strong winds into about 4 in of water and   was submerged for less than 30 seconds before I  retrieved it. This was enough to break the USBC   port completely. Even after drying it out, it  never regained full functionality. No worries,   I thought. It's under warranty. I'll arrange a  walk-in repair. No go. Google does not have a   single repair shop in the whole state of Tasmania.  This means I had to send it in for exchange,   which Google claims takes about 2 weeks to  complete. Well, longer as after waiting 2   hours to chat to Google support, they experience  technical difficulties, meaning I couldn't even   return my phone. In fact, 4 days later, I'm  yet to get word on how I actually send this   in for repair. If this was my only camera and  I relied on it for my work, I'd be completely   after this mishap. I was surprised I'd never been  prompted to purchase the equivalent of Apple Care,   which is called Pixel Care Plus, to protect my  investment, only to find it doesn't exist outside   of the USA. Another fail for the Pixel. So, after  the painful experience of the iPhone sitting on   the ocean floor for 3 hours and the wind taking  my tripod with Pixel into shallow waters, it is   clear which is the more durable device. But beyond  that, unless you're in the USA, without Pixel Care   Plus coverage combined with a painful and lengthy  repair process, I would not invest in the Pixel as   my primary device. Any accident will leave me out  of pocket and out of work for at least 2 weeks,   which is simply not acceptable for a device  that costs the same amount of the iPhone   17 Pro, but without any of the excellent  support and coverage that Apple delivers. At the end of the day, we're looking at two  premium smartphones that can rival some dedicated   cameras when it comes to image quality. It's wild  how far mobile video has come. And on Android,   this is thanks to Motion Cam Pro. For most  people, the decision comes down to platform.   Some prefer Android, others iOS, with the camera  being secondary to this. And whichever way you go,   the video you can capture today is nothing short  of incredible. The important thing is to actually   get out there and shoot something meaningful.  The tools are already in your hands. But for the   filmmakers who treat their phone as a dedicated  camera, the misfits, the rebels, the raw doggers   who want the most control possible, here's where  the paths diverge. If you value ease of use,   extended recording, lightning fast workflow, and  a huge accessory ecosystem with robust device   protection in the case of unfortunate events,  the iPhone 17 Pro and Pro Max is unmatched.   Apple's ISP handles the heavy lifting for you,  delivering a decent image that's quick to cut,   grade, and publish, even if it's not true raw. But  if you value complete control, true raw video with   the most filmic image possible on a smartphone,  internal recording, optical image stabilization   that makes handheld capture possible, albeit  without any protection outside of the USA's Pixel   Care Plus in the case of damaging your device,  Pixel 10 Pro is the winner, provided you're   willing to take on a more demanding workflow  and capture constraints due to its poor GPU   performance and frequent CPU throttle. coupling.  Both phones are incredibly capable and the best   one really depends on what matters to you as a  filmmaker. In fact, after over a month of daily   filming with the Pixel, it is obvious that the  Xiai 15 Ultra excels where the Pixel 10 Pro fails.   With its 1 in sensor and incredible performance  with full support for direct log, my feeling is   this is a much stronger competitor to the iPhone  17 Pro. Overall, there is no doubt it produces a   much better image with Motion Cam Pro. So, let me  know if you'd like to see how it stacks up against   the 17 Pro in a future video. Now, I'm curious  to hear your thoughts in the comments. I always   appreciate hearing and learning from you. Which  do you think looks better and which would you pick   and why, Pixel 10 Pro or the iPhone 17 Pro? If  this video helped, hit like, give it a hype, share   it around. that helps the most, then consider  supporting my work through a YouTube membership,   which gets you access to all sorts of perks,  including source footage or via my affiliate   links, and by purchasing my LUTs. These videos  take a huge personal investment of both time,   money, and elevated stress levels. No sponsors,  just me. And your support makes future videos   possible. Thanks for watching, and I'll see you  in the next one. Bye. I've been trying to complete   this video for over a month now, but the weather  has been awful. So, let's settle this once and   for all. title: Xiaomi 17 Pro Max vs iPhone 17 Pro Max | Full Comparison!
transcript: Hey folks, the Xyomi 17 Pro Max and Apple's iPhone 17 Pro Max stand as two of the most ambitious smartphones of 2025. Each reflecting a very different philosophy while borrowing cues from one another. It is no secret that Shyomi's new flagship draws design inspiration from Apple's Pro Max series. Yet, it does not stop at imitation. Choosing between them is less about which is objectively better and more about which ecosystem and priorities matter most to the user. From the outside, the two phones appear strikingly similar, both featuring flat edges, premium metal frames, and large OLED panels. However, Shyomi has added a striking 2.9 in dynamic back display beside its camera module. An innovation that doubles as a selfie viewfinder and mini interactive screen for widgets, notifications, and even retro style gaming. If paired with Jaomi's optional controller case, Apple has stuck to its minimalist design language, focusing instead on refining the main 6.9 in OLED panel, which delivers an LTPO enabled 120 Hz refresh rate and 3000 nits of peak brightness. Shyomi's 6.9 in AMOLED is nearly identical in resolution and refresh rate, but takes brightness a step further with a peak of 3,500 nits. Photography is another area where both companies push boundaries, though their approaches differ. Shyomi leans on its Leica partnership with a triple 50 megapixel setup, a 102° ultrawide, and a powerful 5x periscope lens that promises excellent optical zoom. Apple counters with its triple 48 megapixel system. Apple's ultrawide offers a broader 120 degree field of view compared to Shyomi's, making it more versatile for landscape and group shots. Early indications suggest Shyomi will lead in zoom versatility and creative photo rendering. While Apple will once again dominate in video quality and lensto lens consistency, performance is evenly matched with both phones equipped with top tier processors. The iPhone 17 Pro Max debuts Apple A19 Pro, supported by a redesigned vapor chamber for improved sustained performance and power efficiency. Apple claims up to 40% gains over its predecessor in demanding tasks. Shyomi equips its flagship with Qualcomm's Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5, which has narrowed the gap with Apple significantly, particularly in GPU and multi-core performance. Battery life is where Jaomi truly sets itself apart. Apple's iPhone 17 Pro Max features a 4,832 MIA hour battery or a slightly larger 5,088 MIA hour cell in a SIM only models, claiming up to 41 hours of video playback, its best endurance yet. Shyomi, however, packs a colossal 7,500 mAh battery into the 17 Pro Max, dwarfing Apple's numbers and virtually every other flagship on the market. Coupled with 100 watt fast charging, it promises not only long-lasting endurance, but also rapid recharging, Apple's more modest 45 watt charging will feel slow by comparison, but benefits from safe, stable, and globally standardized charging. On the software front, the choice is largely dictated by preference. Apple's iOS 26 introduces its most radical visual update in years with the new liquid glass interface, blending translucency and refractions into the UI for a premium look. It also continues to refine multitasking, ecosystem integration, and privacy controls. Shyomi's Hyper OS3 built on Android 16 emphasizes customization, flexibility, and a growing ecosystem of connected devices. Price and availability add further distinction. The iPhone 17 Pro Max launched globally on September 19th, starting at $1,199 for the 256 gigs model. Shyomi 17 Pro Max, by contrast, is available only in China for now at $840, though global pricing will almost certainly be higher when it launches internationally, likely in early 2026. Ultimately, the Shyomi 17 Pro Max is the bolder device, experimenting with new hardware features like the rear dynamic back display and delivering sheer brute force with its oversized battery and rapid charging. The iPhone 17 Pro Max, on the other hand, focuses on refinement, ecosystem cohesion, and pushing forward Apple's vision of design and software harmony. For Android enthusiasts who want maximum specs and features, the Shyomi is the clear alternative. For iPhone users, the 17 Pro Max is the most compelling upgrade Apple has delivered in years. Both stand as pinnacle devices of their ecosystems, and the real deciding factor comes down to whether you live in Apple's walled garden or Shyomi's expanding Android universe. Thanks for watching. If you have any questions, I look forward to them in the comments. Have a nice day and God bless you. [Music]

title: Apple’s Cosmic Orange iPhone 17 Pro Turning Pink, Users Report | Firstpost America
transcript: Now, it was supposed to be Apple's most striking color yet. A bold, fiery, cosmic orange that screamed both innovation and style. But it seems the iPhone 17 Pro lineup didn't quite read the brief. Just weeks after launch, that bright orange has reportedly started to fade to pink. You heard that right, pink. Photos on X and Reddit show the fading frame sparking questions about whether this is a design flaw, a cleaning mishap, or simply an unplanned shade shift. In our final story, we look at what might be turning Apple's latest showpiece from a design statement into an unexpected science experiment. >> Apple's latest hero color may be losing some of its shine, quite literally. Just weeks after launch, the iPhone 17 Pro and iPhone Pro Max in the eye-catching cosmic orange shade are reportedly developing an unexpected pinkish tint, leaving some users wondering whether their phones are blushing or malfunctioning. Images flooding social media platforms like X and Reddit show the once vibrant orange devices taking on a noticeably pink hue. While some have brushed it off as a trick of light, others are less amused, particularly those who paid a premium for Apple's latest flagship model. Unlike its titanium predecessors, the iPhone 17 Pro series features an anodized aluminum frame. It is a design shift that allows Apple to achieve richer colors such as the now infamous cosmic orange. But there's a catch. According to reports, anodized aluminium is more porous. This means the dye that gives it its dazzling color can also make it more susceptible to external chemical reactions. In simple terms, the same process that lets Apple paint the town orange might also be turning it pink. Anodized aluminium can fade or discolor when it comes into contact with hydrogen peroxide. It is a compound commonly found in cleaning sprays, sanitizers, and even beauty products. The chemical can reportedly damage the sealed dye layer, leading to a pinkish or uneven fade over time. Apple's own support page has long warned against cleaning devices with products containing bleach or hydrogen peroxide. It recommends instead the use of 70% isopropyl alcohol or 75% ethyl alcohol wipes. The company also advises against letting moisture seep into device openings or submerging iPhones in any cleaning solutions. Tech experts, meanwhile, are urging users to go easy on cleaning agents and keep their shiny new phones far from cosmetic products and household chemicals. The pink predicament comes on the heels of another controversy nicknamed Scratchgate. Shortly after the iPhone 17 Pro's release, users began complaining that the devices anodized aluminium body, meant to be lighter than last year's titanium design, was scratching far too easily. This was happening especially in darker shades like deep blue. With complaints about scratches, fading hues, and chemical reactions, Apple's decision to switch materials may be proving to be a double-edged sword. or in this case, a double stained surface. For many fans, the Cosmic Orange iPhone 17 Pro remains a bold and beautiful choice, a statement piece that turns heads wherever it goes. But for others, the color's unexpected evolution from orange to pink feels less cosmic and more of a cosmetic mishap until Apple steps in with an explanation or a fix. Users might have to embrace the unintended gradient look. After all, in the world of premium tech fashion, maybe pink really is the new orange. >> Want the facts? >> The latest developments, >> news that gets straight to the point. >> Well, we've got all three just for you. This is First Post Live, a brand new show. Your window into what really matters. >> Don't miss it.

Yes, the phone is more prone to scratches, but the unibody design, or the fact that the whole device is a single cast alloy, with no parts assembled together, and the back of the phone being entirely glass (which would make it more prone to breakage), is all metal except for that rectangular section made of Ceramic Shield, a very strong layer resistant to shocks, scratches, drops, and all that. But I still put a protective layer on that. And although on paper this phone weighs more than the 16 Pro Max and is also thicker, when you hold the two phones  You'll feel that this phone is lighter, or lighter in the hand. I don't know if the weight distribution here is a bit better, or if what caused the thickness to be higher is the camera area, which protrudes more than last year. But overall, for a more luxurious grip, I'd go with the 16 Pro Max, but for a more comfortable grip and a lighter feel, I'd choose the 17 Pro Max. One of the strange things I noticed this year is that the screen bezels are larger. I don't know if we're moving forward or backward. Look at the two screens side by side. I don't know if you'll notice what I'm not, but the 16 Pro Max has a 91.4% screen-to-body ratio, while the 17 Pro Max is at 90.7%. I do n't know why, but I just noticed the difference. For me, this is a downgrade, not an upgrade. The real upgrade is in the actual screen brightness.  Under sunlight, which practically translates to watching content, browsing, or generally using your phone in the sun, if most of your work is outdoors, this will be much more comfortable on the 17 Pro Max screen. We're talking about 3000 nits compared to 2000 nits last year. And there's also an anti-reflective coating. You might ask, what's that? Well, the screen here has a layer that reduces reflections. Any strong light source hitting the screen is supposed to absorb or significantly reduce it. But compared to other phones with the same coating, their performance is much better than this one. In fact, once I installed a screen protector, I practically lost this advantage. As you can see, I have a screen protector on both the iPhone 16 Pro Max and the 17 Pro Max, and of course, anyone buying one of these phones should install one.  The screen protector isn't noticeable; both reflect light normally. But let's forget about the screen and all that superficial stuff. Let's talk about the actual hardware. Last year it was the A18 Pro, and this year it's the A19 Pro. Naturally, as we've come to expect, Apple releases the most powerful processors on the market until Qualcomm releases its next version, and then Apple surpasses them. The A19 Pro is achieving terrifying numbers—we're talking about 2.6 million points, and on version 11, 3 million points, and so on. We're hearing about millions of points now, even on phones we hold in our hands and play PUBG on. So we don't really need to talk about the processor. What we need to talk about is the new software, iOS 26, which we were thrilled to see officially released on the iPhone 17 Pro Max and starting to roll out to other devices. Therefore, I had updated it to  Hey Titanium, you were using the iPhone on the 16 Pro Max before I even got the 17, and I was experiencing some lag and glitches in apps. The animations were a bit clunky, or the movement wasn't smooth enough. So I decided to put that aside. The 17 Pro Max is what will solve these problems for me. Honestly, it solved the animation issue; you can tell the software was really designed and optimized for it. But my problem is still with Apple's app optimizations after iOS 18. Since then, I don't know what's wrong with my iPhone. I keep getting crashes or freezes in WhatsApp, and it closes by itself. The camera app closes when I'm looking at some pictures, and it closes by itself. The same problem happens with Gmail too; sometimes it freezes on its own. Many apps freeze.  It shuts down on its own. Of course, some people will attack me after saying this, but I'm sure a lot of people experience this with their iPhones. Everyone always denies it to justify the high prices they pay for these phones. But I understand them. All this happening, all these crashing apps, on a phone with 12GB of RAM, which is supposed to be equivalent to 24GB on Android. Of course, we already know that on iOS. But as you know from me, not everything is negative. The positive aspect of this phone, or its performance, is the heat management. Apple has really done a great job this year, thank God. They used fiber chambers, like most Android companies have for a long time. It's not a new invention, as people imagine. And yes, there's water inside the phone, and it can spill. I heard it can spill, but guys, vapor or liquid cooling has been around for a very long time.  Android has been around for five or six years, but the fact that Apple finally started using it this year makes a huge difference in heat distribution in the phone. The liquid chamber absorbs the heat; the liquid inside absorbs this heat and evaporates, and the metal piece on top of the liquid distributes the heat throughout the phone. So, the heat isn't concentrated in one spot and burns your hand like it did with the 16 Pro Max. I actually tested the temperature of both phones for over an hour, and I deliberately left the cases on. After half an hour of testing and recording video at 4K 60 frames per second, the 17 Pro Max had the advantage in cooling by more than two degrees. I'm not talking about the device as a whole, but about the components on the back of the processor and the screen. The screen also got hotter, and there was a noticeable difference in the 16 Pro Max's heat distribution. The 17 Pro Max distributed the heat much better.  After a while, and after half an hour, the temperature of both devices started to decrease, and the phones began to get used to my work. I ran a Gigabench and 3D Market benchmark to heat up the graphics processor and the phone's processor, and then I went back to recording 4K at 60 frames per second. The 17 Pro Max maintained its temperature, always exceeding it by two degrees or more compared to the 16 Pro Max. Incidentally, the 16 Pro Max reached insane temperatures I've never seen before. The phone was literally burning me while I was monitoring and controlling the heat, which is the primary enemy of battery life. The more the phone heats up, the more power it consumes, thus draining the battery. And thankfully, the battery is quite large. We were approaching 5000 mAh and then surpassed it, reaching 5088 mAh on the version that comes with a SIM card (not a physical SIM). And honestly...  The version I prefer means forget about traditional SIM cards. Get the iSIM for 300 EGP. You can buy it and put more than 10 lines on your phone, which is much better than a traditional SIM. Battery performance is one of the things that will impress you the most if you're thinking of upgrading from the 15 Pro Max or 16 Pro Max to the 17 Pro Max. Anyone who owns the 16 Pro Max will understand what I'm saying. You know how it is when you take your phone out all day and find it's about to give you the last hour or two, and you're like, "Just give me those two hours and I'll charge you, okay?" But it just shuts down. The 17 Pro Max, on the other hand, gives you those two hours. This is thanks to the better power-saving processor, the new power modes Apple has added, the larger battery (which is the most important thing), and the reduced heat – something you'll also notice.  Regarding charging speed, the first 50% charge on both the iPhone 17 Pro Max and iPhone 17 Pro Max takes approximately 20 minutes or 21 minutes, while the iPhone 16 Pro Max took about half an hour, sometimes longer. However, one of the strange things, which I mentioned in a short post a while ago, is that Apple used a new Wi-Fi modem in the iPhone 17 Pro Max, calling it Apple N1 or Apple Wi-Fi 7, whatever the name is. This meant sacrificing the Qualcomm modem used for receiving Wi-Fi networks, which, frankly, had a negative impact on me after testing it multiple times. The Wi-Fi speeds are consistently much faster on the iPhone 16 Pro Max than on the iPhone 17 Pro Max. If you're close to the router, the difference is more than 100 Mbps, and if you're farther away, it's only 20 to 30 Mbps. That's almost double the speed in certain locations or at certain times, which is still considered a significant drop.  A review of the previous generation: Let's count how many downgrades we've made so far. We've downgraded materials and Wi-Fi. There's one thing we haven't talked about, and it's something everyone's been waiting for: the cameras and photography. My dear brother, for those who don't want to waste their time on camera talk, let me summarize the story so you understand the extent of the improvement between the 17 Pro Max and the 16 Pro Max. I want to tell you that the main camera in both produces the same images. The same details, the same processing, the same everything, literally. I challenge anyone to find any differences between them unless you zoom in and wait for about five minutes, then ask your friends and everyone around you what the differences are between these two photos. The differences are really, really, really difficult for anyone to find. I can't find any differences except very rarely. For example, the face here might be slightly redder, and the face there might have slightly less contrast. So, there are some things...  It's not that I was going crazy trying to find differences between them, and that's not surprising since they both have the same sensors. I just expected a slightly better processing or some change, but that didn't happen. The same goes for the UltraD camera; both are very good and excellent in every way, but they both have the same sensor, the same shooting experience, the same details, the same everything. So, we're left with the telephoto and selfie cameras. Regarding the telephoto camera, which is specifically for zoom, you have a completely new 48-megapixel camera that gives you 4x optical zoom without sacrificing any quality. It also has 8x zoom, but that cuts through the sensor, so of course, we'll be sacrificing detail. This sensor, which used to produce really, really good photos with a 100mm focal length, made the images crisp and detailed, with excellent bokeh. The focal length was ideal for portraits and people; even the bokeh was practically flawless. And the skin...  The skin tone is beautiful, which is what we've come to expect from Apple. Plus, you also get 40x zoom, which is more than the zoom on the 15 Pro Max. We're getting closer and closer, year by year, to the 100x zoom other companies have been offering for the past three years. The truth is, this year's telephoto is better, but when I put the portrait samples from both phones side-by-side, the differences, as you can see, are almost negligible. However, when you're photographing, for example, a building, the sharpness is slightly more pronounced on the 17 Pro Max. But when it comes to portrait photography, the differences are very, very, very small; it's difficult for an average person to notice them. Besides, the 16 Pro Max has a 120mm focal length, so you need to move much further away from your subject. Personally, I prefer 4x; it feels more reasonable. I don't have to go to the ends of the earth. To take a picture, I mean, but to be honest, the best thing about this phone, and what would make you, especially if you're a content creator, switch, is the selfie camera. This 18-megapixel selfie camera has the square sensor we've all talked about more than once, and I'm sure you know it better than I do. With a square sensor, you no longer need to flip the phone sideways if you have friends in the picture. The phone simply cuts a portion of the frame or the large sensor and produces a wide, sharp landscape photo full of detail with decent contrast—everything you could dream of in a selfie camera on a phone. It's truly one of the widest and best selfie cameras I've ever tried. But if you ask me about image quality or sharpness, and the overall look of the photos between it and the 16 Pro Max, aside from the wider field of view and the larger frame, the differences are very, very, very slight. You'll find in some areas  The shadow feature is completely gone on the 16 Pro Max, but the 17 Pro Max is trying to bring it back. These are the only differences I noticed between the two phones. There's also a new feature called Dual Capture. I don't know why it's not included on the 16 Pro Max. What's stopping you from taking pictures with the rear camera and the selfie camera simultaneously? Like, capturing your reaction while you're shooting something in front of you? I don't understand. We've always seen this on Android and done it with third-party apps. I just download an app and use two cameras at the same time. So why is this update only on the 17 Pro Max and not on the 16 Pro Max, the 15 Pro Max, or even the 13 Pro Max? What's stopping it? I don't understand what the big deal is about having both cameras working at the same time. I've told you the most important developments and the things we've gone backwards with in the 17 Pro Max. So, should you upgrade or not? Honestly, look From my personal perspective, you need two things I'm about to mention. First, you need to have the money readily available. You don't absolutely need the extra amount you'll be adding to your current iPhone model. That's essential, of course, but if you're going to borrow and take out loans and all that, and end up with, say, the 15 Pro Max or the 16 Pro Max, then I don't see any point in adding 30 more, and there's no point in paying for it in installments. Second, if your current iPhone's battery is bothering you, then just end the video and go for the 17 Pro Max. The battery upgrade difference is truly huge, and I'm telling you this with someone who has the 16 Pro Max. Imagine how much better it is for someone with the 15 Pro Max, the 14 Pro Max, or even the 13 Pro Max – the difference is massive! Or, if you're a content creator who makes a lot of vlogs and cares about taking wide-angle selfies and all that, then go for it! Otherwise, in my opinion... Practically speaking, I don't see any other significant differences between the two phones. Upgrades are only possible from the 13 Pro Max or 14 Pro Max, but beyond that, neither the 15 Pro Max nor the 16 Pro Max offer any improvement. That's my opinion. Speaking of content creators, vloggers, and those who care about the selfie camera, I bought this accessory for 1000 Egyptian pounds. I might make a short video about it. You attach it to the back of the phone and use the rear camera to take selfie videos. So don't tell me about new sensors or square sensors; just attach this thing and you'll get amazing selfie quality. That's all for today's video. I hope you enjoyed it and found it helpful. Don't forget to subscribe to the channel because, God willing, I'll be posting comparisons between the 17 Pro Max and any flagship phone that comes out soon. Compare it to the 17 Pro Max, and I'm Omar, in case you don't know me, and I'll see you in a new video.
